question: B-3. Assume that a \( 60^{\circ} \) angle cannot be trisected with ruler and compass alone. Prove that if \( n \) is a positive multiple of 3 , then no angle of \( 360 / n \) degrees can be trisected with ruler and compass alone.
solution:
B-3 We need to make use of the following facts about fields and constructibility: (1) If \( Q \) is the field of rational numbers, the degree of \( Q \) extended by \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / k\right) \), where \( k \) is a positive integer, is \( \phi(k) \), where \( \phi \) is the Euler function. (2) If \( K, L, M \) are fields with \( K \subset L \subset M \) and \( [L: K]<\infty,[M: L]<\infty \) then \( [M: K]=[M: L] \cdot[L: K] \). (3) Given \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / k\right) \), then \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / 3 k\right) \) is constructible if and only if
\[
\left[\varrho\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{3 k}\right): Q\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{k}\right)\right]
\]
is a power of 2 .
Consequently, \( \left[Q\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / 3 k\right): Q\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / k\right)\right] \cdot \phi(k)=\phi(3 k) \). Now
\[
\phi\left(3^{a}\right)=3^{a-1} \cdot 2=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \phi\left(3^{a-1}\right), & \text { if } a>1 \\
2, & \text { if } a=1
\end{array}\right.
\]
and, by the multiplicative property of the Euler function,
\[
\phi(3 k)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \phi(k), & \text { if } 3 \mid k \\
2 \phi(k), & \text { if } 3 \nmid k .
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore an angle of size \( 360^{\circ} / k \) is trisectible if and only if \( 3 \nmid k \).

descriptive_long.question: B-3. Assume that a \( 60^{\circ} \) angle cannot be trisected with ruler and compass alone. Prove that if \( integercount \) is a positive multiple of 3 , then no angle of \( 360 / integercount \) degrees can be trisected with ruler and compass alone.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3 We need to make use of the following facts about fields and constructibility: (1) If \( fieldrationals \) is the field of rational numbers, the degree of \( fieldrationals \) extended by \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / divisorindex\right) \), where \( divisorindex \) is a positive integer, is \( eulerfunction(divisorindex) \), where \( eulerfunction \) is the Euler function. (2) If \( fieldalpha, fieldbeta, fieldgamma \) are fields with \( fieldalpha \subset fieldbeta \subset fieldgamma \) and \( [fieldbeta: fieldalpha]<\infty,[fieldgamma: fieldbeta]<\infty \) then \( [fieldgamma: fieldalpha]=[fieldgamma: fieldbeta] \cdot[fieldbeta: fieldalpha] \). (3) Given \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / divisorindex\right) \), then \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / 3 divisorindex\right) \) is constructible if and only if
\[
\left[rhosymbol\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{3 divisorindex}\right): fieldrationals\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{divisorindex}\right)\right]
\]
is a power of 2 .
Consequently, \( \left[fieldrationals\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / 3 divisorindex\right): fieldrationals\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / divisorindex\right)\right] \cdot eulerfunction(divisorindex)=eulerfunction(3 divisorindex) \). Now
\[
eulerfunction\left(3^{exponentvalue}\right)=3^{exponentvalue-1} \cdot 2=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, eulerfunction\left(3^{exponentvalue-1}\right), & \text { if } exponentvalue>1 \\
2, & \text { if } exponentvalue=1
\end{array}\right.
\]
and, by the multiplicative property of the Euler function,
\[
eulerfunction(3 divisorindex)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, eulerfunction(divisorindex), & \text { if } 3 \mid divisorindex \\
2 \, eulerfunction(divisorindex), & \text { if } 3 \nmid divisorindex .
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore an angle of size \( 360^{\circ} / divisorindex \) is trisectible if and only if \( 3 \nmid divisorindex \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-3. Assume that a \( 60^{\circ} \) angle cannot be trisected with ruler and compass alone. Prove that if \( watermelon \) is a positive multiple of 3 , then no angle of \( 360 / watermelon \) degrees can be trisected with ruler and compass alone.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3 We need to make use of the following facts about fields and constructibility: (1) If \( umbrella \) is the field of rational numbers, the degree of \( umbrella \) extended by \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / blueberry\right) \), where \( blueberry \) is a positive integer, is \( jellybean(blueberry) \), where \( jellybean \) is the Euler function. (2) If \( elephantine, hummingbird, toothpick \) are fields with \( elephantine \subset hummingbird \subset toothpick \) and \( [hummingbird: elephantine]<\infty,[toothpick: hummingbird]<\infty \) then \( [toothpick: elephantine]=[toothpick: hummingbird] \cdot[hummingbird: elephantine] \). (3) Given \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / blueberry\right) \), then \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / 3 blueberry\right) \) is constructible if and only if
\[
\left[marshmallow\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{3 blueberry}\right): umbrella\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{blueberry}\right)\right]
\]
is a power of 2 .
Consequently, \( \left[umbrella\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / 3 blueberry\right): umbrella\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / blueberry\right)\right] \cdot jellybean(blueberry)=jellybean(3 blueberry) \). Now
\[
jellybean\left(3^{pineapple}\right)=3^{pineapple-1} \cdot 2=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, jellybean\left(3^{pineapple-1}\right), & \text { if } pineapple>1 \\
2, & \text { if } pineapple=1
\end{array}\right.
\]
and, by the multiplicative property of the Euler function,
\[
jellybean(3 blueberry)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, jellybean(blueberry), & \text { if } 3 \mid blueberry \\
2 \, jellybean(blueberry), & \text { if } 3 \nmid blueberry .
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore an angle of size \( 360^{\circ} / blueberry \) is trisectible if and only if \( 3 \nmid blueberry \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-3. Assume that a \( 60^{\circ} \) angle cannot be trisected with ruler and compass alone. Prove that if \( negativemultiple \) is a positive multiple of 3 , then no angle of \( 360 / negativemultiple \) degrees can be trisected with ruler and compass alone.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3 We need to make use of the following facts about fields and constructibility: (1) If \( irrationalfield \) is the field of rational numbers, the degree of \( irrationalfield \) extended by \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / nonmultiple\right) \), where \( nonmultiple \) is a positive integer, is \( noncoprimecount(nonmultiple) \), where \( noncoprimecount \) is the Euler function. (2) If \( oversetfield, exteriorfield, subfield \) are fields with \( oversetfield \subset exteriorfield \subset subfield \) and \( [exteriorfield: oversetfield]<\infty,[subfield: exteriorfield]<\infty \) then \( [subfield: oversetfield]=[subfield: exteriorfield] \cdot[exteriorfield: oversetfield] \). (3) Given \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / nonmultiple\right) \), then \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / 3 nonmultiple\right) \) is constructible if and only if
\[
\left[imprecisionsym\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{3 nonmultiple}\right): irrationalfield\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{nonmultiple}\right)\right]
\]
is a power of 2 .
Consequently, \( \left[irrationalfield\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / 3 nonmultiple\right): irrationalfield\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / nonmultiple\right)\right] \cdot noncoprimecount(nonmultiple)=noncoprimecount(3 nonmultiple) \). Now
\[
noncoprimecount\left(3^{unboundedexponent}\right)=3^{unboundedexponent-1} \cdot 2=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 noncoprimecount\left(3^{unboundedexponent-1}\right), & \text { if } unboundedexponent>1 \\
2, & \text { if } unboundedexponent=1
\end{array}\right.
\]
and, by the multiplicative property of the Euler function,
\[
noncoprimecount(3 nonmultiple)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 noncoprimecount(nonmultiple), & \text { if } 3 \mid nonmultiple \\
2 noncoprimecount(nonmultiple), & \text { if } 3 \nmid nonmultiple .
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore an angle of size \( 360^{\circ} / nonmultiple \) is trisectible if and only if \( 3 \nmid nonmultiple \).

garbled_string.question: Assume that a \( 60^{\circ} \) angle cannot be trisected with ruler and compass alone. Prove that if qlskvtrm is a positive multiple of 3 , then no angle of 360 / qlskvtrm degrees can be trisected with ruler and compass alone.
garbled_string.solution:
B-3 We need to make use of the following facts about fields and constructibility: (1) If \( xesmyolp \) is the field of rational numbers, the degree of \( xesmyolp \) extended by \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / zpwfrdqc\right) \), where \( zpwfrdqc \) is a positive integer, is \( uvyhjprs(zpwfrdqc) \), where \( uvyhjprs \) is the Euler function. (2) If \( dfgplunk, hqzovtjr, nbqwirca \) are fields with \( dfgplunk \subset hqzovtjr \subset nbqwirca \) and \( [hqzovtjr: dfgplunk]<\infty,[nbqwirca: hqzovtjr]<\infty \) then \( [nbqwirca: dfgplunk]=[nbqwirca: hqzovtjr] \cdot[hqzovtjr: dfgplunk] \). (3) Given \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / zpwfrdqc\right) \), then \( \cos \left(360^{\circ} / 3 zpwfrdqc\right) \) is constructible if and only if
\[
\left[bczlwnmd\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{3 zpwfrdqc}\right): xesmyolp\left(\cos \frac{360^{\circ}}{zpwfrdqc}\right)\right]
\]
is a power of 2 . Consequently, \( \left[xesmyolp\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / 3 zpwfrdqc\right): xesmyolp\left(\cos 360^{\circ} / zpwfrdqc\right)\right] \cdot uvyhjprs(zpwfrdqc)=uvyhjprs(3 zpwfrdqc) \). Now
\[
uvyhjprs\left(3^{gmxoehva}\right)=3^{gmxoehva-1} \cdot 2=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, uvyhjprs\left(3^{gmxoehva-1}\right), & \text { if } gmxoehva>1 \\
2, & \text { if } gmxoehva=1
\end{array}\right.
\]
and, by the multiplicative property of the Euler function,
\[
uvyhjprs(3 zpwfrdqc)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
3 \, uvyhjprs(zpwfrdqc), & \text { if } 3 \mid zpwfrdqc \\
2 \, uvyhjprs(zpwfrdqc), & \text { if } 3 \nmid zpwfrdqc .
\end{array}\right.
\]

Therefore an angle of size \( 360^{\circ} / zpwfrdqc \) is trisectible if and only if \( 3 \nmid zpwfrdqc \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $p\ge 5$ be a fixed odd prime.  
Write every positive integer $n$ uniquely as $n=p^{k}m$ with $k\ge 0$ and $p\nmid m$, and put $\displaystyle\theta_{n}:=\dfrac{2\pi}{n}$.

1.  Arithmetic of real cyclotomic fields.  
   For $s\ge 1$ set  
   \[
        K_{s}:=\mathbb{Q}\!\bigl(\cos(2\pi/s)\bigr)\subset\mathbb{R}.
   \]

   (a)  Prove that $K_{s}=\mathbb{Q}$ if and only if $s\in\{1,2,3,4,6\}$.

   (b)  Show that for every $k\ge 1$
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{k}m}:K_{p^{\,k-1}m}\bigr]=
            \begin{cases}
               \dfrac{p-1}{2}, &\text{if }k=1\text{ and }m\in\{1,2\},\\[6pt]
               p-1,            &\text{if }k=1\text{ and }m\notin\{1,2\},\\[6pt]
               p,              &\text{if }k\ge 2.
            \end{cases}\tag{$\star$}
        \]
        In particular,
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}\bigr]=p\qquad(k\ge 1).\tag{$\star\star$}
        \]

2.  $p$-section of the angle $\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.  

   (a)  Fix $k\ge 0$ and set  
        \[
            \beta:=\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m},\qquad    
            \alpha:=\cos\!\Bigl(\beta/p\Bigr)=\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr).
        \]
        Prove that
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{\,k+1}m}\bigr]=p.\tag{$\ddagger$}
        \]

   (b)  Deduce that $\beta$ cannot be $p$-sected with straight-edge and compass, because any Euclidean construction produces only towers of quadratic extensions.

   (c)  Show further that for $k\ge 1$
        \[
            K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha)=K_{p^{\,k+2}m},
            \qquad
            \bigl[K_{p^{k}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{k}m}\bigr]=p^{2}.
        \]
        Explain why starting the construction from the smaller field $K_{p^{k}m}$ yields the degree $p^{2}$, thereby clarifying the frequent pitfall of using an inappropriate base field.

3.  Galois-theoretic interpretation.    

   (a)  Put $\zeta_{s}:=e^{2\pi i/s}$ and identify  
        \[
            G_{k}:=\operatorname{Gal}\!\bigl(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{p^{k}m})/\mathbb{Q}\bigr)
                  \simeq(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}.
        \]
        For $k\ge 1$ define  
        \[
            H_{k}:=\bigl\{x\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}\;:\;
                      x\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k-1}m}\bigr\}.
        \]
        Show that $|H_{1}|=p-1$ while $|H_{k}|=p$ for $k\ge 2$.

   (b)  Prove that for every $k\ge 1$ the fixed field of $H_{k}$ inside the maximal real subfield $K_{p^{k}m}$ is exactly $K_{p^{\,k-1}m}$.

   (c)  Using (b) with $k\mapsto k+2$, confirm $(\ddagger)$ and the non-constructibility statement in 2(b).

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $n=p^{k}m$ with $p\nmid m$, put  
$\zeta_{s}=e^{2\pi i/s}$, and recall  
\[
   K_{s}=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}+\zeta_{s}^{-1})
        \subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}),\qquad
   \bigl[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}):\mathbb{Q}\bigr]=\varphi(s).
\]
Because complex conjugation has fixed field $K_{s}$,  
\[
   [K_{s}:\mathbb{Q}]
        =
   \begin{cases}
        \dfrac{\varphi(s)}{2},&s\notin\{1,2,3,4,6\},\\[4pt]
        1,&s\in\{1,2,3,4,6\}.
   \end{cases}\tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Proof of $(\star)$ and $(\star\star)$.

Since $(p,m)=1$ we have  
\[
   \varphi(p^{k}m)=p^{k-1}(p-1)\varphi(m).
\]
Insert this into (1) for $k\ge 1$ to obtain  
\[
   [K_{p^{k}m}:\mathbb{Q}]
        =\dfrac{p^{\,k-1}(p-1)\varphi(m)}{2}.\tag{2}
\]

(a)  The case $k=1$.  

If $m\in\{1,2\}$ then $\varphi(m)=1$ and $K_{m}=\mathbb{Q}$, so  
\[
   [K_{pm}:K_{m}]
      =[K_{pm}:\mathbb{Q}]
      =\dfrac{p-1}{2}.
\]

If $m\notin\{1,2\}$, then either $m\in\{3,4,6\}$ (for which $K_{m}=\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varphi(m)=2$) or $m\ge 5$ (for which $[K_{m}:\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi(m)/2$).  
In both sub-cases
\[
   [K_{pm}:K_{m}]
      =\dfrac{\tfrac12(p-1)\varphi(m)}{[K_{m}:\mathbb{Q}]}
      =p-1.
\]

(b)  The case $k\ge 2$.  

Dividing consecutive degrees given by (2) we obtain
\[
   [K_{p^{k}m}:K_{p^{\,k-1}m}]
      =\dfrac{p^{\,k-1}(p-1)}{2}\bigg/\dfrac{p^{\,k-2}(p-1)}{2}=p,
\]
which is the third line of $(\star)$.  With $k\ge 1$ this yields
\[
   [K_{p^{k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]=p,\tag{$\star\star$}
\]
establishing $(\star\star)$ in its correct range.  
\qed

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The $p$-section problem.

Fix $k\ge 0$ and put  
\[
   \beta:=\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m},\qquad
   \alpha:=\cos\!\Bigl(\beta/p\Bigr)=\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr)
   \in K_{p^{\,k+2}m}.
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(a).  Degree computation over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

Because $k+1\ge 1$, $(\star\star)$ gives  
\[
   \bigl[K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{\,k+1}m}\bigr]=p.\tag{3}
\]

We prove that $\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ by exhibiting an automorphism
of $K_{p^{\,k+2}m}$ that fixes $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ but moves $\alpha$.

Let  
\[
   \sigma:\zeta_{p^{\,k+2}m}\longmapsto
          \zeta_{p^{\,k+2}m}^{\,1+p^{\,k+1}m}.
\]
Because $1+p^{\,k+1}m\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k+1}m}$, the automorphism $\sigma$
acts trivially on $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

However,
\[
   \sigma(\alpha)
      =\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{1+p^{\,k+1}m}{p^{\,k+2}m}\,2\pi\Bigr)
      =\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}+\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\Bigr)
      \neq\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr)=\alpha,
\]
because $p\ge 5$ implies $\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\not\equiv 0\pmod{2\pi}$.
Thus $\alpha$ is not fixed by $\sigma$ and therefore
$\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

Consequently the minimal polynomial of $\alpha$ over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$
has degree divisible by
\[
   |\langle\sigma\rangle|=p.
\]
Since the whole extension in (3) already has degree $p$, the degree equals
$p$, whence
\[
   [K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{\,k+1}m}]=p,
\]
establishing $(\ddagger)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(b).  Non-constructibility.

Any straight-edge-and-compass construction starting from
$K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ yields fields obtained by successive quadratic
extensions.  Hence every constructible element lies in a tower whose
total degree over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ is a power of $2$.
Because $(\ddagger)$ furnishes an odd prime divisor $p$ of that degree,
$\alpha$ (equivalently $\beta/p$) is not constructible, and the angle
$\beta$ cannot be $p$-sected.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(c).  Working over the smaller field $K_{p^{k}m}$ ($k\ge 1$).

Using $(\star\star)$ twice we get
\[
   [K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]
      =[K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{\,k+1}m}]\,
       [K_{p^{\,k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]
      =p\cdot p=p^{2}.
\]
Because $\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ it certainly is not in
$K_{p^{k}m}$, so its degree over $K_{p^{k}m}$ is the full $p^{2}$ and
\[
   K_{p^{k}m}(\alpha)=K_{p^{\,k+2}m}.
\]
This confirms the necessity of choosing the correct base field when
computing degrees in constructibility questions.
\qed

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Galois-theoretic explanation.

Let
\[
   L_{k}:=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{p^{k}m}),\qquad
   G_{k}:=\operatorname{Gal}(L_{k}/\mathbb{Q})
         \simeq(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times},
\]
and denote complex conjugation by $\iota$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(a).  The subgroups $H_{k}$.

Reduction modulo $p^{\,k-1}m$ gives a surjection
\[
   \rho_{k}:G_{k}\longrightarrow(\mathbb{Z}/p^{\,k-1}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}
\]
whose kernel is
\[
   H_{k}=\ker\rho_{k}
        =\{x\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k-1}m}\}.
\]
A direct count shows $|H_{1}|=p-1$ and $|H_{k}|=p$ for $k\ge 2$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(b).  Fixed fields.

Inside the maximal real subfield
$K_{p^{k}m}=L_{k}^{\langle\iota\rangle}$ consider the subgroup
\[
   H_{k}\langle\iota\rangle/\langle\iota\rangle
      \;\le\;\operatorname{Gal}(K_{p^{k}m}/\mathbb{Q}).
\]
For $k\ge 2$ this subgroup has order $p$, so its fixed field is an
index-$p$ subfield of $K_{p^{k}m}$; by $(\star)$ this fixed field is
$K_{p^{\,k-1}m}$.  For $k=1$ an analogous argument yields the two
possible indices $\dfrac{p-1}{2}$ and $p-1$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(c).  Verification of $(\ddagger)$.

Replacing $k$ by $k+2$ in (b) shows that
$K_{p^{\,k+2}m}$ is the unique index-$p$ extension of $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$
inside $L_{k+2}$.  Since $\alpha$ is moved by the automorphism $\sigma$
from Section 2(a), it lies outside $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ and therefore
generates that unique degree-$p$ extension, confirming $(\ddagger)$ and
again precluding constructibility.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Consequences.

If $p\ge 5$ divides $n$, then the angle $\theta_{n}=2\pi/n$ is not
$p$-sectible.  Multiplying $n$ by any power of $2$ does not affect this
obstruction, because it is encoded in the $p$-primary part of the
associated real cyclotomic field.  
\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher-order prime powers.  
    The original problems dealt with a single prime divisor of n.  Here the statement must hold simultaneously for every power pᵏ dividing n, and the proof must track how the obstruction propagates up the tower n↦pn↦p²n↦… .

2.  Precise index computation.  
    One must compute the exact degree of the field extension (★), not merely show that it is divisible by p.  This requires explicit use of the Euler-totient formula and of cyclotomic-field Galois theory rather than the elementary degree-counting used in the original solution.

3.  Explicit Galois-group subgroup.  
    The solver must identify the subgroup Gal(L_k/L_{k−1}) inside (ℤ/pᵏmℤ)×, relate it to the real subfield via complex conjugation, and prove that its order forbids constructibility.  Thus the argument demands familiarity with the interplay between complex conjugation, real subfields, and subgroup indices—topics absent from the original problem.

4.  Robust obstruction.  
    The enhanced statement shows that adding any number of quadratic extensions (i.e., adjoining as many “power-of-2 steps” as one wishes) can never overcome the prime obstruction.  This calls for a deeper appreciation of constructibility than the binary “possible/impossible” conclusion of the original problem.

5.  Multiple interacting concepts.  
    The solution intertwines cyclotomic fields, Euler’s totient function, real subfields, constructibility criteria, subgroup indices, and the incompatibility of odd primes with powers of two, thereby demanding a broader arsenal of algebraic-number-theoretic tools than the original or kernel variants.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $p\ge 5$ be a fixed odd prime.  
Write every positive integer $n$ uniquely as $n=p^{k}m$ with $k\ge 0$ and $p\nmid m$, and put $\displaystyle\theta_{n}:=\dfrac{2\pi}{n}$.

1.  Arithmetic of real cyclotomic fields.  
   For $s\ge 1$ set  
   \[
        K_{s}:=\mathbb{Q}\!\bigl(\cos(2\pi/s)\bigr)\subset\mathbb{R}.
   \]

   (a)  Prove that $K_{s}=\mathbb{Q}$ if and only if $s\in\{1,2,3,4,6\}$.

   (b)  Show that for every $k\ge 1$
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{k}m}:K_{p^{\,k-1}m}\bigr]=
            \begin{cases}
               \dfrac{p-1}{2}, &\text{if }k=1\text{ and }m\in\{1,2\},\\[6pt]
               p-1,            &\text{if }k=1\text{ and }m\notin\{1,2\},\\[6pt]
               p,              &\text{if }k\ge 2.
            \end{cases}\tag{$\star$}
        \]
        In particular,
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}\bigr]=p\qquad(k\ge 1).\tag{$\star\star$}
        \]

2.  $p$-section of the angle $\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.  

   (a)  Fix $k\ge 0$ and set  
        \[
            \beta:=\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m},\qquad    
            \alpha:=\cos\!\Bigl(\beta/p\Bigr)=\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr).
        \]
        Prove that
        \[
            \bigl[K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{\,k+1}m}\bigr]=p.\tag{$\ddagger$}
        \]

   (b)  Deduce that $\beta$ cannot be $p$-sected with straight-edge and compass, because any Euclidean construction produces only towers of quadratic extensions.

   (c)  Show further that for $k\ge 1$
        \[
            K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha)=K_{p^{\,k+2}m},
            \qquad
            \bigl[K_{p^{k}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{k}m}\bigr]=p^{2}.
        \]
        Explain why starting the construction from the smaller field $K_{p^{k}m}$ yields the degree $p^{2}$, thereby clarifying the frequent pitfall of using an inappropriate base field.

3.  Galois-theoretic interpretation.    

   (a)  Put $\zeta_{s}:=e^{2\pi i/s}$ and identify  
        \[
            G_{k}:=\operatorname{Gal}\!\bigl(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{p^{k}m})/\mathbb{Q}\bigr)
                  \simeq(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}.
        \]
        For $k\ge 1$ define  
        \[
            H_{k}:=\bigl\{x\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}\;:\;
                      x\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k-1}m}\bigr\}.
        \]
        Show that $|H_{1}|=p-1$ while $|H_{k}|=p$ for $k\ge 2$.

   (b)  Prove that for every $k\ge 1$ the fixed field of $H_{k}$ inside the maximal real subfield $K_{p^{k}m}$ is exactly $K_{p^{\,k-1}m}$.

   (c)  Using (b) with $k\mapsto k+2$, confirm $(\ddagger)$ and the non-constructibility statement in 2(b).

\bigskip
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $n=p^{k}m$ with $p\nmid m$, put  
$\zeta_{s}=e^{2\pi i/s}$, and recall  
\[
   K_{s}=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}+\zeta_{s}^{-1})
        \subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}),\qquad
   \bigl[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{s}):\mathbb{Q}\bigr]=\varphi(s).
\]
Because complex conjugation has fixed field $K_{s}$,  
\[
   [K_{s}:\mathbb{Q}]
        =
   \begin{cases}
        \dfrac{\varphi(s)}{2},&s\notin\{1,2,3,4,6\},\\[4pt]
        1,&s\in\{1,2,3,4,6\}.
   \end{cases}\tag{1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Proof of $(\star)$ and $(\star\star)$.

Since $(p,m)=1$ we have  
\[
   \varphi(p^{k}m)=p^{k-1}(p-1)\varphi(m).
\]
Insert this into (1) for $k\ge 1$ to obtain  
\[
   [K_{p^{k}m}:\mathbb{Q}]
        =\dfrac{p^{\,k-1}(p-1)\varphi(m)}{2}.\tag{2}
\]

(a)  The case $k=1$.  

If $m\in\{1,2\}$ then $\varphi(m)=1$ and $K_{m}=\mathbb{Q}$, so  
\[
   [K_{pm}:K_{m}]
      =[K_{pm}:\mathbb{Q}]
      =\dfrac{p-1}{2}.
\]

If $m\notin\{1,2\}$, then either $m\in\{3,4,6\}$ (for which $K_{m}=\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varphi(m)=2$) or $m\ge 5$ (for which $[K_{m}:\mathbb{Q}]=\varphi(m)/2$).  
In both sub-cases
\[
   [K_{pm}:K_{m}]
      =\dfrac{\tfrac12(p-1)\varphi(m)}{[K_{m}:\mathbb{Q}]}
      =p-1.
\]

(b)  The case $k\ge 2$.  

Dividing consecutive degrees given by (2) we obtain
\[
   [K_{p^{k}m}:K_{p^{\,k-1}m}]
      =\dfrac{p^{\,k-1}(p-1)}{2}\bigg/\dfrac{p^{\,k-2}(p-1)}{2}=p,
\]
which is the third line of $(\star)$.  With $k\ge 1$ this yields
\[
   [K_{p^{k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]=p,\tag{$\star\star$}
\]
establishing $(\star\star)$ in its correct range.  
\qed

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The $p$-section problem.

Fix $k\ge 0$ and put  
\[
   \beta:=\theta_{p^{\,k+1}m},\qquad
   \alpha:=\cos\!\Bigl(\beta/p\Bigr)=\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr)
   \in K_{p^{\,k+2}m}.
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(a).  Degree computation over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

Because $k+1\ge 1$, $(\star\star)$ gives  
\[
   \bigl[K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{\,k+1}m}\bigr]=p.\tag{3}
\]

We prove that $\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ by exhibiting an automorphism
of $K_{p^{\,k+2}m}$ that fixes $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ but moves $\alpha$.

Let  
\[
   \sigma:\zeta_{p^{\,k+2}m}\longmapsto
          \zeta_{p^{\,k+2}m}^{\,1+p^{\,k+1}m}.
\]
Because $1+p^{\,k+1}m\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k+1}m}$, the automorphism $\sigma$
acts trivially on $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

However,
\[
   \sigma(\alpha)
      =\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{1+p^{\,k+1}m}{p^{\,k+2}m}\,2\pi\Bigr)
      =\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}+\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\Bigr)
      \neq\cos\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\pi}{p^{\,k+2}m}\Bigr)=\alpha,
\]
because $p\ge 5$ implies $\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\not\equiv 0\pmod{2\pi}$.
Thus $\alpha$ is not fixed by $\sigma$ and therefore
$\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$.

Consequently the minimal polynomial of $\alpha$ over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$
has degree divisible by
\[
   |\langle\sigma\rangle|=p.
\]
Since the whole extension in (3) already has degree $p$, the degree equals
$p$, whence
\[
   [K_{p^{\,k+1}m}(\alpha):K_{p^{\,k+1}m}]=p,
\]
establishing $(\ddagger)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(b).  Non-constructibility.

Any straight-edge-and-compass construction starting from
$K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ yields fields obtained by successive quadratic
extensions.  Hence every constructible element lies in a tower whose
total degree over $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ is a power of $2$.
Because $(\ddagger)$ furnishes an odd prime divisor $p$ of that degree,
$\alpha$ (equivalently $\beta/p$) is not constructible, and the angle
$\beta$ cannot be $p$-sected.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2(c).  Working over the smaller field $K_{p^{k}m}$ ($k\ge 1$).

Using $(\star\star)$ twice we get
\[
   [K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]
      =[K_{p^{\,k+2}m}:K_{p^{\,k+1}m}]\,
       [K_{p^{\,k+1}m}:K_{p^{k}m}]
      =p\cdot p=p^{2}.
\]
Because $\alpha\notin K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ it certainly is not in
$K_{p^{k}m}$, so its degree over $K_{p^{k}m}$ is the full $p^{2}$ and
\[
   K_{p^{k}m}(\alpha)=K_{p^{\,k+2}m}.
\]
This confirms the necessity of choosing the correct base field when
computing degrees in constructibility questions.
\qed

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Galois-theoretic explanation.

Let
\[
   L_{k}:=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{p^{k}m}),\qquad
   G_{k}:=\operatorname{Gal}(L_{k}/\mathbb{Q})
         \simeq(\mathbb{Z}/p^{k}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times},
\]
and denote complex conjugation by $\iota$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(a).  The subgroups $H_{k}$.

Reduction modulo $p^{\,k-1}m$ gives a surjection
\[
   \rho_{k}:G_{k}\longrightarrow(\mathbb{Z}/p^{\,k-1}m\mathbb{Z})^{\times}
\]
whose kernel is
\[
   H_{k}=\ker\rho_{k}
        =\{x\equiv 1\pmod{p^{\,k-1}m}\}.
\]
A direct count shows $|H_{1}|=p-1$ and $|H_{k}|=p$ for $k\ge 2$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(b).  Fixed fields.

Inside the maximal real subfield
$K_{p^{k}m}=L_{k}^{\langle\iota\rangle}$ consider the subgroup
\[
   H_{k}\langle\iota\rangle/\langle\iota\rangle
      \;\le\;\operatorname{Gal}(K_{p^{k}m}/\mathbb{Q}).
\]
For $k\ge 2$ this subgroup has order $p$, so its fixed field is an
index-$p$ subfield of $K_{p^{k}m}$; by $(\star)$ this fixed field is
$K_{p^{\,k-1}m}$.  For $k=1$ an analogous argument yields the two
possible indices $\dfrac{p-1}{2}$ and $p-1$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3(c).  Verification of $(\ddagger)$.

Replacing $k$ by $k+2$ in (b) shows that
$K_{p^{\,k+2}m}$ is the unique index-$p$ extension of $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$
inside $L_{k+2}$.  Since $\alpha$ is moved by the automorphism $\sigma$
from Section 2(a), it lies outside $K_{p^{\,k+1}m}$ and therefore
generates that unique degree-$p$ extension, confirming $(\ddagger)$ and
again precluding constructibility.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Consequences.

If $p\ge 5$ divides $n$, then the angle $\theta_{n}=2\pi/n$ is not
$p$-sectible.  Multiplying $n$ by any power of $2$ does not affect this
obstruction, because it is encoded in the $p$-primary part of the
associated real cyclotomic field.  
\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher-order prime powers.  
    The original problems dealt with a single prime divisor of n.  Here the statement must hold simultaneously for every power pᵏ dividing n, and the proof must track how the obstruction propagates up the tower n↦pn↦p²n↦… .

2.  Precise index computation.  
    One must compute the exact degree of the field extension (★), not merely show that it is divisible by p.  This requires explicit use of the Euler-totient formula and of cyclotomic-field Galois theory rather than the elementary degree-counting used in the original solution.

3.  Explicit Galois-group subgroup.  
    The solver must identify the subgroup Gal(L_k/L_{k−1}) inside (ℤ/pᵏmℤ)×, relate it to the real subfield via complex conjugation, and prove that its order forbids constructibility.  Thus the argument demands familiarity with the interplay between complex conjugation, real subfields, and subgroup indices—topics absent from the original problem.

4.  Robust obstruction.  
    The enhanced statement shows that adding any number of quadratic extensions (i.e., adjoining as many “power-of-2 steps” as one wishes) can never overcome the prime obstruction.  This calls for a deeper appreciation of constructibility than the binary “possible/impossible” conclusion of the original problem.

5.  Multiple interacting concepts.  
    The solution intertwines cyclotomic fields, Euler’s totient function, real subfields, constructibility criteria, subgroup indices, and the incompatibility of odd primes with powers of two, thereby demanding a broader arsenal of algebraic-number-theoretic tools than the original or kernel variants.
